1208	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — D1: the presheaf pullback Lan decomposition
1209	
1210	These are general `PresheafOfModules` statements (any `F : C ⥤ D`, ring presheaves
1211	`R, S`), not specific to schemes; they are project-local because Mathlib's pinned commit
1212	exposes neither `extendScalars` nor `pullback₀` at the presheaf-of-modules level. Both are
1213	realised as the left adjoint of an existing right adjoint: `pushforward₀ F R` is
1214	definitionally `pushforward (𝟙 (F.op ⋙ R))` (because `restrictScalars (𝟙) = 𝟭` on the nose,
1215	witnessed by Mathlib's `restrictScalars (𝟙 R)).Full := inferInstanceAs (𝟭 _).Full`), and
1216	`restrictScalars φ` is definitionally `pushforward (F := 𝟭) φ`; since `pushforward _` is
1217	always a right adjoint, so are these two, and their left adjoints `pullback₀`/`extendScalars`
1218	exist. The decomposition `pullback φ ≅ extendScalars φ ⋙ pullback₀` then follows from the
1219	definitional factorisation `pushforward φ = pushforward₀ F R ⋙ restrictScalars φ` by
1220	uniqueness of left adjoints (`Adjunction.leftAdjointCompIso`).
1221	
1222	Per blueprint `lem:pullback_lan_decomposition` (D1). **OFF-PATH (iter-243 pivot).** D1 is
1223	axiom-clean and self-contained, but it was the first brick of the ABANDONED general
1224	strong-monoidal pullback build (`sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality`, route (e)); the active
1225	route is the loc-triv chart-chase D1'–D4' (§below), which does NOT consume `extendScalars`/
1226	`pullback₀`. Retained as a correct, reusable presheaf-level decomposition; do NOT extend it
1227	toward the general build (D2 scalar-strong / D3 topological-interchange are NOT pursued). -/
1228	
1229	section PullbackLanDecomposition
1230	
1231	variable {C : Type u} [Category.{u} C] {D : Type u} [Category.{u} D]
1232	  {F : C ⥤ D} {R : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat.{u}} {S : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat.{u}}
1233	
1234	/-- `pushforward₀ F R` is a right adjoint: it is definitionally `pushforward (𝟙 (F.op ⋙ R))`
1235	(since `restrictScalars (𝟙) = 𝟭` on the nose). Project-local; carries the existence of the
1236	topological inverse image `pullback₀`. -/
1237	private lemma pushforward₀IsRightAdjoint (F : C ⥤ D) (R : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat.{u}) :
1238	    (PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀.{u} F R).IsRightAdjoint :=
1239	  inferInstanceAs (PresheafOfModules.pushforward.{u} (𝟙 (F.op ⋙ R))).IsRightAdjoint
1240	
1241	/-- `restrictScalars φ` is a right adjoint: it is definitionally `pushforward (F := 𝟭) φ`.
1242	Project-local; carries the existence of the extension of scalars `extendScalars`. -/
1243	private lemma restrictScalarsIsRightAdjoint (φ : S ⟶ F.op ⋙ R) :
1244	    (PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars.{u} φ).IsRightAdjoint :=
1245	  inferInstanceAs
1246	    (PresheafOfModules.pushforward.{u} (F := 𝟭 C) (R := F.op ⋙ R) φ).IsRightAdjoint
1247	
1248	/-- **The topological inverse image `pullback₀ := (pushforward₀ F R).leftAdjoint`**, the
1249	left adjoint of the fixed-ring pushforward. On underlying presheaves it is the left Kan
1250	extension along `F.op`. Project-local (no `PresheafOfModules` inverse-image functor at the
1251	pin); the carrier of D3. -/
1252	noncomputable def pullback0 (F : C ⥤ D) (R : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat.{u}) :
1253	    _root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (F.op ⋙ R) ⥤ _root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} R :=
1254	  haveI := pushforward₀IsRightAdjoint F R
1255	  (PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀ F R).leftAdjoint
1256	
1257	/-- **Extension of scalars `extendScalars φ := (restrictScalars φ).leftAdjoint`**, the left
1258	adjoint of restriction of scalars along a morphism of ring presheaves. Project-local; the
1259	carrier of D2 (strong monoidal via `distribBaseChange`). -/
1260	noncomputable def extendScalars (φ : S ⟶ F.op ⋙ R) :
1261	    _root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} S ⥤ _root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (F.op ⋙ R) :=
1262	  haveI := restrictScalarsIsRightAdjoint φ
1263	  (PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars φ).leftAdjoint
1264	
1265	/-- The adjunction `pullback₀ ⊣ pushforward₀`. -/
1266	noncomputable def pullback0Adjunction (F : C ⥤ D) (R : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat.{u}) :
1267	    pullback0 F R ⊣ PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀ F R :=
1268	  haveI := pushforward₀IsRightAdjoint F R
1269	  Adjunction.ofIsRightAdjoint (PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀ F R)
1270	
1271	/-- The adjunction `extendScalars φ ⊣ restrictScalars φ`. -/
1272	noncomputable def extendScalarsAdjunction (φ : S ⟶ F.op ⋙ R) :
1273	    extendScalars φ ⊣ PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars φ :=
1274	  haveI := restrictScalarsIsRightAdjoint φ
1275	  Adjunction.ofIsRightAdjoint (PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars φ)
1276	
1277	/-- **D1 — the presheaf pullback Lan decomposition** (blueprint `lem:pullback_lan_decomposition`).
1278	For `φ : S ⟶ F.op ⋙ R` presenting a pushforward of presheaves of modules, the presheaf
1279	pullback factors as extension of scalars followed by the topological inverse image,
1280	`pullback φ ≅ extendScalars φ ⋙ pullback₀`. This is the left-adjoint reversal of the
1281	definitional factorisation `pushforward φ = pushforward₀ F R ⋙ restrictScalars φ`, obtained
1282	from `Adjunction.leftAdjointCompIso` (uniqueness of left adjoints). Project-local. -/
1283	noncomputable def pullbackLanDecomposition (φ : S ⟶ F.op ⋙ R) :
1284	    PresheafOfModules.pullback φ ≅ extendScalars φ ⋙ pullback0 F R :=
1285	  (Adjunction.leftAdjointCompIso
1286	    (extendScalarsAdjunction φ) (pullback0Adjunction F R)
1287	    (PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction φ)
1288	    (Iso.refl (PresheafOfModules.pushforward φ))).symm
1289	
1290	end PullbackLanDecomposition
1291

1293

1295	`pullbackTensorMap` (`f^*(M ⊗ N) ⟶ f^*M ⊗ f^*N`) to an isomorphism, blueprint
1296	§`sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality`, sub-lemmas D1'–D4'. -/

1298	section LocTrivPullbackTensor
1299	
1300	/-- **The sheafified `⊗ₘ` of the two `pullbackValIso`s (piece 4 of `pullbackTensorMap`) is an

1304	private lemma isIso_sheafify_tensorHom_pullbackValIso {X Y : Scheme.{u}}
1305	    (f : Y ⟶ X) (M N : X.Modules) :
1306	    IsIso ((PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := Y.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 Y.ringCatSheaf.val)).map
1307	      (MonoidalCategory.tensorHom
1308	        (C := _root_.PresheafOfModules (Y.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))
1309	        ((SheafOfModules.forget Y.ringCatSheaf).map (pullbackValIso f M).hom)

1312	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorIso

1317	/-- **Reduction of `pullbackTensorMap` iso-ness to the sheafified presheaf δ.**
1318	
1319	`pullbackTensorMap f M N` is the four-fold composite
1320	`(sheafificationCompPullback).hom ≫ a_Y.map δ ≫ (sheafifyTensorUnitIso).hom ≫ a_Y.map (tensorHom …)`.
1321	Three of the four factors (`sheafificationCompPullback`, `sheafifyTensorUnitIso`,
1322	and the `tensorHom` of the two `pullbackValIso`s) are isomorphisms unconditionally;
1323	the only conditional factor is the sheafification `a_Y.map δ` of the presheaf-level
1324	oplax comparison `δ`. Hence `pullbackTensorMap f M N` is an iso whenever that
1325	sheafified δ is an iso. This isolates the SOLE remaining content (the sheafified δ)

1327	lemma isIso_pullbackTensorMap_of_isIso_sheafifyDelta {X Y : Scheme.{u}}
1328	    (f : Y ⟶ X) (M N : X.Modules)
1329	    (h : letI φ' : (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) ⟶
1330	          (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map f.base).op ⋙ (Y.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) :=
1331	          (f.toRingCatSheafHom).hom
1332	        IsIso ((PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := Y.ringCatSheaf)
1333	          (𝟙 Y.ringCatSheaf.val)).map
1334	          (Functor.OplaxMonoidal.δ (PresheafOfModules.pullback φ') M.val N.val))) :
1335	    IsIso (pullbackTensorMap f M N) := by
1336	  unfold pullbackTensorMap
1337	  extract_lets φ φ' a_Y
1338	  -- piece 2 (the sheafified δ) is the only conditional factor — supplied by `h`.
1339	  haveI hδ : IsIso (a_Y.map
1340	      (Functor.OplaxMonoidal.δ (PresheafOfModules.pullback φ') M.val N.val)) := h
1341	  -- pieces 1 and 3 are `Iso.hom`s; piece 4 is `a_Y.map` of the (iso) `⊗ₘ` of the two
1342	  -- `pullbackValIso`s, supplied by the factored top-level helper.
1343	  exact IsIso.comp_isIso' inferInstance (IsIso.comp_isIso' hδ
1344	    (IsIso.comp_isIso' inferInstance (isIso_sheafify_tensorHom_pullbackValIso f M N)))

1346	/-- **Converse of `isIso_sheafification_map_of_W`.** A morphism of presheaves of modules whose
1347	image under the associated-sheaf functor is an isomorphism lies (on underlying additive presheaves)
1348	in the sheafification localizer `J.W`. This is the same morphism-property identity
1349	`PresheafOfModules.inverseImage_W_toPresheaf_eq_inverseImage_isomorphisms` (the sheafification
1350	functor *is* the localization at `J.W.inverseImage (toPresheaf _)`) read backwards. Project-local:
1351	needed to feed the flatness-free whiskering lemmas `W_whisker{Left,Right}_of_W` from a sheafified
1352	iso (the D2' η-bridge route, below). -/
1353	private lemma W_of_isIso_sheafification {C : Type u} [Category.{u} C] {J : GrothendieckTopology C}
1354	    {R₀ : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat} {Rsh : CategoryTheory.Sheaf J RingCat} (α : R₀ ⟶ Rsh.obj)
1355	    [Presheaf.IsLocallyInjective J α] [Presheaf.IsLocallySurjective J α]
1356	    [J.WEqualsLocallyBijective AddCommGrpCat] [CategoryTheory.HasWeakSheafify J AddCommGrpCat]
1357	    {A B : _root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} R₀} (f : A ⟶ B)

1359	    J.W ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf R₀).map f) := by
1360	  have h := PresheafOfModules.inverseImage_W_toPresheaf_eq_inverseImage_isomorphisms (J := J) α
1361	  have h2 : (CategoryTheory.MorphismProperty.isomorphisms (SheafOfModules Rsh)).inverseImage
1362	      (PresheafOfModules.sheafification α) f := hf
1363	  rw [← h] at h2

1370	`η (pullback φ')` (right-whiskered by `F.obj 𝟙_`) and the (iso) left unitor. Sheafifying, the
1371	unitor factor and the `F.map (λ_ 𝟙_)` factor are isomorphisms unconditionally; the whiskered
1372	`η`-factor `a_Y.map (η F ▷ F.obj 𝟙_)` is an iso whenever `a_Y.map (η F)` is — because a sheafified
1373	iso lies in `J.W` (`W_of_isIso_sheafification`), `J.W` is stable under right-whiskering

1378	self-contained modulo the η-bridge `IsIso (a_Y.map (η (pullback φ')))`. -/

1380	    (h : letI φ' : (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) ⟶
1381	          (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map f.base).op ⋙ (Y.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) :=
1382	          (f.toRingCatSheafHom).hom

1384	          (Functor.OplaxMonoidal.η (PresheafOfModules.pullback φ')))) :
1385	    letI φ' : (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) ⟶
1386	        (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map f.base).op ⋙ (Y.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) :=
1387	        (f.toRingCatSheafHom).hom

1389	      (Functor.OplaxMonoidal.δ (PresheafOfModules.pullback φ')
1390	        (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf).val (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf).val)) := by
1391	  letI φ' : (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) ⟶
1392	      (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map f.base).op ⋙ (Y.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) :=

1398	      ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf _).map (Functor.OplaxMonoidal.η F)) :=
1399	    W_of_isIso_sheafification (𝟙 Y.ringCatSheaf.val) _ h

1401	      ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf _).map (Functor.OplaxMonoidal.η F ▷ F.obj (𝟙_ _))) :=

1405	  haveI hIsoLam : IsIso (a_Y.map (λ_ (F.obj (𝟙_ _))).hom) := inferInstance
1406	  have hBC : IsIso (a_Y.map
1407	      (Functor.OplaxMonoidal.η F ▷ F.obj (𝟙_ _) ≫ (λ_ (F.obj (𝟙_ _))).hom)) := by

1411	  have key : a_Y.map (Functor.OplaxMonoidal.δ F (𝟙_ _) (𝟙_ _)) ≫
1412	      a_Y.map (Functor.OplaxMonoidal.η F ▷ F.obj (𝟙_ _) ≫ (λ_ (F.obj (𝟙_ _))).hom)

1421	/-- **D2' assembly — `pullbackTensorMap` on the unit pair is an iso, given the η-bridge.**

1428	lemma isIso_pullbackTensorMap_unitPair_of_isIso_sheafifyEta {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : Y ⟶ X)

1441	the η-bridge `IsIso (a_Y.map (η (pullback φ')))` (via `left_unitality_hom` + the W-stable

1449	`a_Y.map (η F) ≫ sheafifyUnitIso.hom = (pullbackValIso f 𝒪_X).hom ≫ pullbackObjUnitToUnit φ`.
1450	Transposing across `SheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction φ` (apply `.homEquiv.injective`,

1453	identity (sheafification-mate bridge):
1454	
1455	  `sheafAdj.unit.app 𝒪_X ≫ (pushforward φ).map ((pullbackValIso).inv ≫
1456	      a_Y.map (pullback_pre.map ε_pre ≫ presheafAdj.counit) ≫ sheafifyUnitIso.hom)
1457	    = unitToPushforwardObjUnit φ`,

1460	compatibility of `SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback`/`pullbackIso` (the bridges inside
1461	`pullbackValIso`) — `Adjunction.{homEquiv_,unit_,}leftAdjointUniq_hom_app`,

1463	unit-side analog of `pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp`, NOT yet assembled (a self-contained next step).

1496	    letI φ' : (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) ⟶
1497	        (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map f.base).op ⋙ (Y.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat) :=

1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean
4	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/StalkTensor.lean
5	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/Vestigial.lean
6	
7	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
8	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
9	
10	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
11	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
12	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
13	\[
14	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
15	  \qquad
16	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
17	\]
18	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
19	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
20	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
21	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
22	
23	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
24	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
25	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
26	% groups)
27	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
28	% SOURCE QUOTE:
29	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
30	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
31	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
32	% abelian groups defined by the formula
33	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
34	% \end{dfn}
35	%  [\dots]
36	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
37	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
39	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
40	% topologies  by
41	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
42	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
43	%  \end{dfn}"
44	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
45	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
46	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
47	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
48	homomorphisms.}
49	
50	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
51	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
52	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
53	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
54	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
55	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
56	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
57	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
58	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
59	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
60	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
61	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
62	structure. This is the content of
63	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
64	
65	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
66	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
67	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
68	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
69	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
70	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
71	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
72	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
73	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
74	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
75	\begin{enumerate}
76	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
77	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
78	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
79	    isomorphism classes;
80	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
81	    for \(\otimes\);
82	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
83	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
84	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
85	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
86	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
87	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
88	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
89	\end{enumerate}
90	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
91	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
92	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
93	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
94	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
95	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
96	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
97	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
98	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
99	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
100	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
101	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
102	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
103	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
104	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
105	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
106	supplied.
107	
108	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
109	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
110	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
111	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
112	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
113	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
114	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
115	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
116	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
117	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
118	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
119	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
120	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
121	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
122	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
123	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
124	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
125	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
126	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
127	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
128	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
129	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
130	avoids. The
131	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
132	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
133	The \emph{full} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent
134	monoidal category is therefore not built for the group law and is off the critical
135	path; what the group law does still need from this circle of ideas is the
136	\emph{existence} of the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), realized as a
137	sheafification transport whose single open left-whisker obligation is closed by the
138	stalk--tensor commutation (d.2). The stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) is accordingly
139	\emph{on} the critical path, while the coherent monoidal category itself is not.
140	
141	\section{Mathlib API survey}
142	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
143	
144	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
145	(\texttt{b80f227}) were originally assembled into a single \emph{realization route}
146	(route~(e)): obtaining a full monoidal structure on the substrate by instantiating
147	Mathlib's abstract localization-of-monoidal-categories API rather than
148	hand-assembling the associator. That route is \emph{superseded}. The group law on
149	iso-classes never consumes a coherent monoidal category, and the associator is
150	obtained by direct gluing through \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, so the
151	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance on which route~(e) hinges is never required.
152	The survey below is retained as a Mathlib-API reference and to record why the route
153	is vestigial; the four Mathlib facts are all verified against the on-disk Mathlib
154	source.
155	
156	\paragraph{(i) The presheaf-of-modules monoidal category.} Mathlib's
157	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal} constructs a
158	monoidal-category instance
159	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\) on
160	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,
161	\mathtt{RingCat})\), with the sectionwise relative tensor
162	\[
163	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
164	\]
165	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct` /
166	% `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`, in
167	% `Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean` (L104, L125)
168	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean).

183	together with associator, left/right unitors and braiding making it symmetric
184	monoidal. The base presheaf of rings \(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,

193	\(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) and the construction
194	\(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\). The class records exactly
195	the whiskering-stability data:
196	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `CategoryTheory.MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal` and
197	% `CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal`, in
198	% `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean` (L44, L86, L440)
199	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean).

241	\texttt{ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal\_W}, in

243	This is the \emph{template} for the proof technique used in route~(e), but it does

248	\paragraph{The gap and route~(e), superseded.} The substrate
249	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) of this chapter is the
250	sheafification of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\), and
251	sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the localization
252	\(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\). Route~(e)
253	proposed to obtain a \emph{full} monoidal structure on

255	by applying \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) (ii) to the already-monoidal category (i),
256	which would require the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (its two
257	whiskering-stability fields \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}, with load-bearing
258	local-injectivity half \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}). This is
259	\emph{not} how the group law is realized. The group law consumes only the
260	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms, and the associator is built
261	directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through
262	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}); it never invokes
263	the \emph{full} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance or a coherent monoidal category.
264	The \emph{full monoidal-category} route~(e) is therefore off the critical path. The
265	associator does, however, consume a \emph{single} left-whisker existence lemma
266	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed by the d.2 stalk--tensor
267	commutation; so the stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) itself is on the critical path, even
268	though the full coherent monoidal category built from it is not.
269	
270	The stalk-infrastructure analysis that route~(e) turned on is recorded here for
271	reference only, since that route is superseded. The \emph{stalk module structure}
272	is already present in Mathlib: the file
273	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk} supplies, for a topological
274	space \(X\), a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) on \(X\) and a point
275	\(x \in X\), the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure on the stalk
276	\(\mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) of a presheaf of modules \(M\),
277	together with the germ--scalar compatibility \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\); a linearity
278	packaging of the induced stalk map of a morphism was built project-side
279	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, the \emph{linearity half} of ingredient (d.1)).
280	Route~(e) additionally required a (d.1)-\emph{bridge} between the site-level
281	\(J.W\) and the topological stalkwise-isomorphism criterion on
282	\(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), and (d.2) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
283	\otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor; there is
284	moreover no monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) in Mathlib. The full \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent monoidal category that route~(e) would
285	build is not pursued; but the (d.1)-linearity packaging and the (d.2) stalk--tensor
286	commutation are \emph{on} the critical path, since the associator's single open
287	left-whisker obligation \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk} is closed
288	precisely by (d.2) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}). What is superseded is only the
289	\emph{pair}-of-whiskering-fields \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) packaging and the
290	\(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category, not the stalk infrastructure.
291	
292	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
293	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
294	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
295	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
296	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
297	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
298	independently of any upstream timing.

300	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
301	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
302	
303	\begin{definition}
304	
305	
306	\leanok
307	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
308	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
310	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
311	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
312	  \[
313	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
314	  \]
315	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
316	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
317	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
318	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
319	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
320	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
321	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
322	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
323	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
324	  canonical compatibility
325	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
326	     \;\cong\;
327	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
328	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
329	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
330	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
331	
332	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
333	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
334	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
335	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
336	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
337	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
338	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
339	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
340	\end{definition}
341	
342	\begin{lemma}
343	
344	
345	\leanok
346	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
347	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
348	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
349	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
350	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
351	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
352	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
353	  \[
354	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
355	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
356	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
357	  \]
358	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
359	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
360	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
361	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
362	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
363	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
364	  \[
365	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
366	    \qquad
367	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
368	  \]
369	  the associator
370	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
371	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
372	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
373	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
374	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
375	\end{lemma}
376	
377	\begin{proof}
378	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
379	  \leanok
380	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
381	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
382	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
383	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
384	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
385	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
386	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
387	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
388	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
389	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
390	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
391	\end{proof}
392	
393	\begin{remark}
394	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
395	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
396	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
397	        lem:whisker_of_W}
398	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
399	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
400	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
401	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
402	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
403	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
404	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
405	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
406	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
407	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
408	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
409	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
410	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
411	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
412	
413	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
414	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
415	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
416	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
417	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
418	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
419	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
420	  fixed-base presheaves in
421	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
422	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
423	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
424	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). The \emph{full}
425	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance and the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal
426	  category are not on the critical path: the group law is realized without them. The
427	  single left-whisker existence lemma and its underlying d.1/d.2 stalk apparatus, by
428	  contrast, \emph{are} on the critical path, since the associator's sheafification
429	  transport consumes exactly that one whisker obligation
430	  (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed via d.2).
431	
432	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
433	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
434	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
435	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
436	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
437	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
438	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
439	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
440	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
441	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
442	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
443	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
444	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
445	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
446	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
447	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
448	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
449	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
450	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
451	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
452	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
453	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
454	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
455	\end{remark}

457	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
458	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
459	
460	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
461	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
462	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
463	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
464	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
465	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
466	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
467	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
468	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
469	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
470	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
471	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
472	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
473	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
474	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
475	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
476	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
477	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
478	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
479	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
480	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
481	to the off-path route~(e).
482	
483	\begin{lemma}
484	
485	\leanok
486	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
487	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
488	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
489	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
490	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
491	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
492	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
493	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
494	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
495	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
496	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
497	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
498	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
499	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
500	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
501	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
502	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
503	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
504	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
505	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
506	  \[
507	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
508	      \;\;:=\;\;
509	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
510	  \]
511	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
512	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
513	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
514	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
515	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
516	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
517	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
518	\end{lemma}
519	
520	\begin{proof}
521	
522	
523	\leanok
524	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
525	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
526	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
527	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
528	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
529	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
530	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
531	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
532	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
533	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
534	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
535	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
536	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
537	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
538	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
539	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
540	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
541	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
542	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
543	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
544	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
545	
546	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
547	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
548	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
549	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
550	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
551	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
552	\end{proof}

554	\begin{lemma}
555	
556	
557	\leanok
558	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
559	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
560	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
561	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
562	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
563	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
564	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
565	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
566	  morphism
567	  \[
568	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
569	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
570	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
571	  \]
572	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
573	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
574	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
575	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
576	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
577	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
578	\end{lemma}
579	
580	\begin{proof}
581	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
582	  \leanok
583	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
584	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
585	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
586	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
587	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
588	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
589	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
590	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
591	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
592	  isomorphism}
593	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
594	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
595	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
596	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
597	  therefore extension of scalars along
598	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
599	
600	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
601	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
602	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
603	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
604	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
605	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
606	  the abstract pullback functor.
607	
608	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
609	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
610	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
611	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
612	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
613	   \;\cong\;

615	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
616	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
617	  comparison
618	  \[

620	      \;\longrightarrow\;
621	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
622	      \;\otimes\;
623	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
624	  \]
625	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).

652	  \texttt{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}: pullback and pushforward of
653	  $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism form an adjoint pair $(f^*, f_*)$, the
654	  abstract universal property on which the comparison below rests.}
655	
656	  \emph{Step 3 (the genuine residual: H1 alone).} Completing this comparison at the
657	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise handle on the abstract functor
658	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
659	  adjoint of pushforward (the presheaf-of-modules avatar of the
660	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) adjunction of the cited Stacks lemma) and admits no
661	  sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the pinned commit. The closure passes through
662	  two presheaf-level ingredients, of which exactly one now remains open.
663	
664	  The first ingredient --- a strong-monoidal upgrade of
665	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-sheaf ring \emph{isomorphism}
666	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so that base change along the isomorphism commutes with
667	  \(\otimes\) --- is \emph{closed in this chapter} as
668	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
669	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}\)): for a ring isomorphism
670	  \(e : R \simeq S\) the canonical map \(a \otimes_R b \mapsto a \otimes_S b\) is an
671	  \(R\)-linear equivalence \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R
672	  \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M
673	  \otimes_S N)\).
674	
675	  The \emph{sole remaining residual} is therefore the presheaf-level identification
676	  \[
677	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \;\cong\; \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
678	  \]
679	  via \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\). One exhibits a
680	  presheaf-level adjunction
681	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) and concludes
682	  that \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\), being a left adjoint of
683	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), is canonically isomorphic to the abstract left
684	  adjoint \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\). Here \(\beta\) is the structure map of the
685	  restriction functor, so that \((M|_f).\mathrm{val} =
686	  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,M.\mathrm{val}\) definitionally;
687	  composing the resulting comparison with
688	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv} yields the asserted isomorphism.
689	
690	  This presheaf-level adjunction is the presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
691	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\), and must be built from
692	  presheaf-level \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
693	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\). These two building blocks are \emph{Mathlib-absent}
694	  at the pinned commit: only the \emph{sheaf}-level versions exist
695	  (\texttt{Mathlib/.../SheafOfModules/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}), whereas
696	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardId}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) do already exist at the
697	  presheaf level. This residual is being constructed \emph{directly}, mirroring the
698	  existing sheaf-level template app-for-app at the presheaf level (a Mathlib-gradient
699	  build, not a deferral to an upstream PR).
700	
701	  This lemma is the single substrate linchpin of the relative Picard group law for
702	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(M, N\): the associator
703	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} consumes the comparison
704	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes_U N|_f\) on its sole consumer
705	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}, where the restriction is commuted
706	  past the \emph{sheafified} tensor \(M \otimes_X N\) \emph{before} any local
707	  trivialisation of \(M, N\) is introduced --- so the comparison is required for the
708	  global, untrivialised modules, and no free-cover specialisation can stand in for it.
709	  Consequently the H1 residual \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong
710	  \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) is genuinely on the critical path, and closing it is
711	  the \emph{single open substrate obligation} of this chapter. The superseded
712	  route~(e) \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to \mathtt{Sheaf.monoidalCategory}\)
713	  (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}) remains named only as a last-resort fallback should the
714	  presheaf-level adjunction itself bottom out; it is not the plan.
715	\end{proof}

717	\begin{lemma}
718	[Presheaf-level pushforward adjunction and strong-monoidal restriction (H1/H2 substrate)]
719	  \label{lem:presheaf_pushforward_adj_substrate}
720	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.pushforwardNatTrans,
721	        PresheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr,
722	        PresheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj,
723	        PresheafOfModules.isIso_of_isIso_app,
724	        PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsMonoidalOfBijective}
725	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
726	  These five presheaf-level declarations are the two ingredients on which the
727	  linchpin \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} rests; both are absent from Mathlib at
728	  the pinned commit and are supplied project-side.
729	
730	  \emph{H1 (the pushforward adjunction).} For a morphism \(\beta\) of presheaves of
731	  rings, \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\) are the
732	  presheaf-level de-sheafifications of Mathlib's sheaf-level
733	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
734	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr}\)
735	  (\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}),
736	  reproduced app-for-app one level down at the presheaf of modules. From them
737	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\) assembles the presheaf-level adjunction
738	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), the
739	  presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
740	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\). Pairing this against the
741	  existing \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}\) through
742	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) yields the canonical isomorphism
743	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) of two left
744	  adjoints of the same pushforward --- exactly the residual identified in Step~3 of
745	  the proof of \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
746	
747	  \emph{H2 (strong-monoidal restriction).} \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_isIso\_app}\) is the
748	  sectionwise isomorphism criterion: a morphism of presheaves of modules is an
749	  isomorphism once each of its section maps is. Built on it,
750	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfBijective}\) equips the presheaf-level
751	  restriction-of-scalars functor
752	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\alpha\) with a \emph{strong}-monoidal
753	  structure whenever the base-change comparison \(\alpha\) is \emph{sectionwise
754	  bijective} --- the presheaf-level lift of the ModuleCat-level H2 core
755	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}, assembled section-by-section via
756	  \(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\). It is to be distinguished from the
757	  module-level, ring-equivalence form
758	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv}\) already pinned in
759	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}: the present declaration is the
760	  \emph{presheaf}-level, \emph{bijective}-comparison form, the one the substrate lift
761	  actually consumes (where the comparison map
762	  \((\alpha.\mathtt{app}\,X).\mathtt{hom}\) is a bijective \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)
763	  morphism but is not syntactically a \((\,\cdot\,)\!.\mathtt{toRingHom}\)).
764	
765	  Together H1 and H2 are the two Mathlib-absent ingredients the linchpin needed; they
766	  are self-contained presheaf-level results and are natural upstream-PR candidates.
767	\end{lemma}

769	\begin{lemma}
770	
771	\leanok
772	[Base change along a ring isomorphism commutes with $\otimes$]
773	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
774	  \lean{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}
775	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M, N\) be
776	  \(S\)-modules. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the tensor
777	  product: the canonical map
778	  \[
779	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N)
780	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
781	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \otimes_S N\bigr),
782	    \qquad a \otimes_R b \;\longmapsto\; a \otimes_S b,
783	  \]
784	  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence. Equivalently, \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\) is
785	  \emph{strong} monoidal, not merely lax, when \(e\) is a ring \emph{isomorphism}:
786	  base change along an isomorphism \(R \simeq S\) identifies the two ground rings, so
787	  the lax structure map of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) is invertible. The
788	  forward map is \(R\)-linear, but its inverse \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\)
789	  is only \emph{additive} (it is not \(S\)-linear), so the inverse is constructed as
790	  a homomorphism of additive groups and then verified to be the two-sided inverse of
791	  the forward \(R\)-linear map. This closes the strong-monoidal ingredient of
792	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, leaving the presheaf-level pushforward
793	  adjunction as the sole open residual there.
794	\end{lemma}

796	\begin{lemma}
797	
798	[Strong-monoidal upgrade of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along a ring isomorphism (ModuleCat-level H2 core)]
799	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}
800	  \lean{restrictScalars_isIso_μ, restrictScalars_isIso_ε,
801	        restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv,
802	        restrictScalars_isIso_μ_of_bijective,
803	        restrictScalars_isIso_ε_of_bijective}
804	  \uses{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
805	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings and let
806	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom} :
807	  \mathtt{ModuleCat}\,S \to \mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R\) be the associated
808	  restriction-of-scalars functor, equipped with its canonical \emph{lax}-monoidal
809	  structure (the lax tensorator \(\mu\) and lax unit \(\varepsilon\) supplied by
810	  Mathlib). Then:
811	  \begin{enumerate}
812	    \item the lax tensorator
813	      \(\mu_{M, N} : \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e M \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e N
814	      \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M \otimes_S N)\) is an isomorphism
815	      (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\mu}\));
816	    \item the lax unit
817	      \(\varepsilon : \mathbf{1}_R \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(\mathbf{1}_S)\)
818	      is an isomorphism (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\varepsilon}\));
819	    \item consequently \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}\) is
820	      \emph{strong} monoidal: it carries the packaged structure
821	      \((\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
822	      (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv}\)).
823	  \end{enumerate}
824	  The same two invertibility statements hold, more flexibly, under the hypothesis that
825	  the underlying ring homomorphism is merely \emph{bijective} rather than literally of
826	  the form \(e.\mathtt{toRingHom}\) for a packaged ring equivalence \(e\)
827	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\mu\_of\_bijective}\),
828	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\varepsilon\_of\_bijective}\)); these bijective
829	  forms are the ones consumed by the presheaf-level lift, where the relevant
830	  comparison map \((\alpha.\mathtt{app}\,X).\mathtt{hom}\) is a bijective
831	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) morphism but is not syntactically a
832	  \((\,\cdot\,)\!.\mathtt{toRingHom}\).
833	\end{lemma}
834	
835	\begin{proof}
836	  \uses{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
837	  Statements (1) and (2) are the categorical repackaging of
838	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}: when the ground-ring map is a ring
839	  \emph{isomorphism} the two ground rings are identified, so the lax structure maps
840	  \(\mu\) and \(\varepsilon\) of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) --- which are
841	  in general only one-directional --- become invertible. For (1) the inverse of
842	  \(\mu_{M, N}\) is the additive map \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\) of
843	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}, verified to be two-sided inverse to
844	  the canonical \(R\)-linear \(\mu_{M, N}\); for (2) the unit comparison
845	  \(\mathbf{1}_R = R \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e S\) is the additive identification
846	  induced by \(e\). Strong monoidality (3) is then the Mathlib upgrade of a
847	  lax-monoidal functor all of whose structure maps are isomorphisms
848	  (\(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\)), packaging \(\mu^{-1}, \varepsilon^{-1}\)
849	  into \((\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}).\mathtt{Monoidal}\).
850	  The bijective-hypothesis forms are proved identically, replacing ``\(e\) is a ring
851	  equivalence'' by ``the underlying ring homomorphism is bijective'' and reconstructing
852	  the inverse ground-ring map from bijectivity; this is what lets the presheaf-level
853	  lift feed in a section-wise-bijective comparison map directly.
854	
855	  These declarations constitute the \emph{ModuleCat-level H2}: strong
856	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along a ring isomorphism commutes with \(\otimes\). The
857	  one \emph{remaining} H2 step is the bounded, no-Mathlib-gap \emph{presheaf} lift:
858	  assembling \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\alpha).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
859	  section-by-section via \(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\), using a
860	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-iso\,\(\Rightarrow\)\,bijective bridge to invoke the
861	  bijective forms above at each section, together with the
862	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) iso-iff-app-iso reflection to lift section-wise
863	  invertibility to the presheaf level; once that lift is in place,
864	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) is monoidal. This presheaf H2 lift is bounded and
865	  has no Mathlib-absent ingredient; the sole genuinely Mathlib-absent piece of
866	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is the presheaf-level pushforward adjunction
867	  (``H1'').
868	\end{proof}

870	\begin{lemma}
871	
872	
873	\leanok
874	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
875	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
876	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
877	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
878	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
879	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
880	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
881	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
882	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
883	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
884	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
885	\end{lemma}
886	
887	\begin{proof}
888	
889	
890	\leanok
891	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
892	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
893	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
894	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
895	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
896	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
897	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
898	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
899	\end{proof}

902	\emph{is} formalized and sorry-free; it is not, however, consumed by the relative

904	\emph{unconditionally} through the varying-ring stalk--tensor commutation

907	obligation for \emph{all} modules with no flatness and no local-triviality

910	self-contained result about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms,

914	\begin{lemma}
915	% Closed, sorry-free standalone result (flat whiskering). Off the critical path:
916	% the associator is realized via the d.2 stalk-tensor route
917	% (lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk), not via this flat lemma.
918	
919	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
920	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
921	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
922	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
923	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
924	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
925	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
926	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
927	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
928	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
929	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
930	  left-whiskered morphism
931	  \[
932	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
933	  \]
934	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
935	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
936	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
937	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
938	
939	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
940	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
941	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
942	  however, \emph{off the critical path}: the live whiskering-stability obligation
943	  for the unconditional associator is closed for an \emph{arbitrary} whiskering
944	  object \(F\) by the stalk--tensor commutation
945	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
946	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}), which never invokes the
947	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of the present lemma. The sectionwise flatness
948	  condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over \(R(U)\) for \emph{every} object \(U\) of the
949	  site, including non-affine opens --- is not supplied by \(\otimes\)-invertibility
950	  or local triviality, which are affine-local properties; the present (flat) lemma
951	  is therefore retained as an independent, closed result, superseded on the critical
952	  path by the d.2 route of \cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}.
953	\end{lemma}
954	
955	\begin{proof}
956	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
957	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
958	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
959	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
960	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
961	
962	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
963	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
964	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
965	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
966	  \[
967	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
968	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
969	  \]
970	
971	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
972	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
973	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
974	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
975	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
976	
977	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
978	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
979	  linear maps (Mathlib
980	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
981	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
982	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
983	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
984	
985	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
986	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
987	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
988	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
989	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
990	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
991	\end{proof}

1004	\begin{lemma}

1007	\leanok
1008	[Sheafification inverts a localizer morphism of presheaves of modules]
1009	  \label{lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W}
1010	  Let \(R_0\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a
1011	  Grothendieck topology \(J\), let \(R_{sh}\) be its sheafification, and let
1012	  \(\alpha : R_0 \to R_{sh}.\mathrm{obj}\) be the (locally bijective) comparison
1013	  map of presheaves of rings. Let \(f : A \to B\) be a morphism of presheaves of
1014	  \(R_0\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
1015	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0).\mathrm{map}\,f\) lies in the sheafification
1016	  localizer class \(J.W\) --- equivalently, is locally bijective for \(J\). Then
1017	  the presheaf-of-modules sheafification functor
1018	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\,\alpha\) sends \(f\) to an
1019	  isomorphism:
1020	  \[
1021	    (\mathtt{sheafification}\,\alpha).\mathrm{map}\,f
1022	    \quad\text{is an isomorphism.}
1023	  \]
1024	  This is a closed go/no-go bridge --- it converts ``\,a morphism lies in
1025	  \(J.W\)\,'' into ``\,its sheafification is invertible\,''. Under route~(e) the
1026	  associator is the API-derived one (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}), so this bridge is
1027	  retained as a supplement rather than as the associator's load-bearing step.
1028	\end{lemma}
1029	
1030	\begin{proof}
1031	
1032	\leanok

1121	[The $R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x$-linear stalk map of a morphism of presheaves of modules]
1122	  \label{lem:stalk_linear_map}
1123	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1124	  Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(R\) a presheaf of commutative rings on
1125	  \(X\), and \(x \in X\) a point. For a morphism \(g : M \to N\) of presheaves of
1126	  \(R\)-modules, the induced map on stalks
1127	  \[
1128	    g_x \;\colon\; M_x \;\longrightarrow\; N_x
1129	  \]
1130	  is \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\)), and is
1131	  characterised on germs by
1132	  \(g_x(\mathrm{germ}_x\,s) = \mathrm{germ}_x(g_U\,s)\)
1133	  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_germ}\)). When the underlying map of
1134	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-valued stalks is an isomorphism, \(g_x\) is bijective
1135	  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\)); in that case it bundles to
1136	  an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear equivalence
1137	  \(M_x \xrightarrow{\sim} N_x\) (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearEquivOfIsIso}\)).
1138	
1139	  These four declarations are the \emph{(d.1)-partial implementation} --- the
1140	  \(R_x\)-linearity packaging of stalk maps --- consumed by the
1141	  \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step in the proofs of
1142	  \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation} and
1143	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}. They build on the
1144	  stalk module structure of \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk};
1145	  the linearity packaging itself is project-original.
1146	\end{lemma}

1148	\begin{proof}
1149	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1150	  The stalk \(M_x = \mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) carries its
1151	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure from
1152	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk}. The underlying additive map
1153	  \(g_x\) on stalks is the filtered colimit of the section maps \(g_U\); since
1154	  each section map is \(R(U)\)-linear and the scalar action on the stalk is the
1155	  colimit of the section actions, \(g_x\) respects the
1156	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-action, which gives the linear map together with its
1157	  germ characterisation. Bijectivity is read off the underlying
1158	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk isomorphism (a bijective linear map is a linear
1159	  equivalence), and bundling the inverse yields the linear equivalence.
1160	\end{proof}

1218	  \emph{PRIMARY route (\(F\) locally trivial --- the consumer's case; sheaf-level,
1219	  no stalks, no (d.2)).} Suppose \(F\) is locally trivial
1220	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), which is exactly the hypothesis its
1221	  sole consumer --- the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- supplies.
1222	  \(J\)-local injectivity of a morphism is \emph{local on a covering family}: it
1223	  suffices to produce a cover of \(X\) on each member of which \(F \triangleleft g\)
1224	  restricts to a locally injective morphism. Take a trivialising open cover
1225	  \(\{V\}\) of \(X\) for \(F\), so that \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) on each \(V\).
1226	  On such a \(V\):
1227	  \begin{itemize}
1228	    \item the substrate--restriction compatibility \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1229	      gives a canonical isomorphism
1230	      \[
1231	        (F \triangleleft g)\big|_V \;\cong\; (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)
1232	      \]
1233	      --- restriction along the open immersion \(V \hookrightarrow X\) commutes with
1234	      \(\otimes\), and the whiskering \(F \triangleleft g = \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g\)
1235	      is \(\otimes\) with an identity, so it restricts to \(\mathrm{id}_{F|_V}
1236	      \otimes (g|_V) = (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)\);
1237	    \item the trivialisation \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) together with the left
1238	      unitor \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_V \otimes_V (-) \cong
1239	      (-)\)) identifies \((F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V) \cong g|_V\).
1240	  \end{itemize}
1241	  Composing, \((F \triangleleft g)|_V\) is isomorphic to \(g|_V\). Now \(g \in J.W\),
1242	  and membership in \(J.W\) (local bijectivity) is stable under restriction along an
1243	  open immersion, so \(g|_V \in J.W\); in particular \(g|_V\) is \(J\)-locally
1244	  injective. An isomorphism preserves local injectivity, hence \((F \triangleleft
1245	  g)|_V\) is locally injective on \(V\). As the \(\{V\}\) cover \(X\) and local
1246	  injectivity is local on the cover, \(F \triangleleft g\) is \(J\)-locally
1247	  injective. \emph{No stalks and no (d.2) stalk--tensor commutation enter}; the
1248	  single comparison lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (a presheaf-level
1249	  comparison, decomposed into its two open-immersion base-change halves) carries the
1250	  argument, and thereby moves \emph{onto} the critical path.
1251

1253	  --- only injective --- and stalls on a \(\mathrm{Tor}_1\)/flatness obligation. Here
1254	  the argument is sheaf-level and uses that \(g\) is locally \emph{bijective} (a
1255	  \(J.W\) morphism), reduced through the unitor to \(g|_V\) (again a \(J.W\)
1256	  morphism); a mere injection is never tensored, so no flatness is needed.
1257	
1258	  \emph{FALLBACK route (arbitrary \(F\); stalkwise, required only for the
1259	  full-generality route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path).} For an
1260	  \emph{arbitrary} (not necessarily locally trivial) \(F\), local injectivity is

1263	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}), ported to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). A
1264	  morphism in \(J.W\) is, on the topological site of \(X\), a \emph{stalkwise
1265	  isomorphism}: at each point \(x\) the induced map on stalks \(g_x\) is an
1266	  isomorphism. The presheaf-of-modules tensor is sectionwise, so it commutes with
1267	  the stalk (a filtered colimit), giving
1268	  \[
1269	    (F \triangleleft g)_x
1270	      \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes_{R_x} g_x
1271	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \;\longrightarrow\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .
1272	  \]
1273	  Tensoring an isomorphism \(g_x\) by the identity \(\mathrm{id}_{F_x}\) yields an
1274	  isomorphism --- no flatness is needed, since \(g_x\) is invertible, not merely
1275	  injective. Hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, in particular
1276	  locally bijective, in particular locally injective, for \emph{any} \(F\).
