1437	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ (unconditional)]
1438	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
1439	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso}
1440	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,
1441	        lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
1442	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
1443	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). There is an isomorphism
1444	  \[
1445	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
1446	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1447	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
1448	  \]
1449	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), natural in \(M, N, P\). The group law on
1450	  \(\otimes\)-iso-classes (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) consumes only
1451	  the \emph{existence} of this isomorphism, the proposition
1452	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \cong M \otimes_X (N
1453	  \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality coherence of it is
1454	  ever required.
1455	
1456	  The Lean pin is unconditional: it holds for arbitrary
1457	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(M, N, P\), with no local-triviality or
1458	  invertibility hypothesis. (Earlier drafts carried vestigial
1459	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) hypotheses to match the carrier; these were
1460	  dropped once the construction below was seen to use none of them.)
1461	\end{lemma}
1462	
1463	\begin{proof}
1464	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,
1465	  \leanok
1466	        lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
1467	  \textbf{Realization (sheafification transport, unconditional via d.2).} The
1468	  associator is the sheafification transport of the presheaf-of-modules associator
1469	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\), which exists for
1470	  \emph{all} presheaves of modules over the varying ring. The transport requires the
1471	  single left-whisker \(F \triangleleft \mathtt{toSheafify}\) to lie in the
1472	  sheafification localizer \(J.W\) for the relevant \(F\); that single open
1473	  left-whiskering obligation \(\mathtt{isLocallyInjective\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) is
1474	  closed \emph{unconditionally} --- for an arbitrary whiskering object, with no
1475	  flatness and no local triviality --- by the d.2 stalk--tensor commutation
1476	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk} (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}):
1477	  a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, and by the d.2 commutation
1478	  \(\bigl(F \triangleleft g\bigr)_x \cong \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) is again an
1479	  isomorphism, so \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). Hence the associator is itself
1480	  unconditional and axiom-clean once d.2 (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}) is
1481	  formalized; it does \emph{not} require the full \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
1482	  or the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category --- only the one left-whisker
1483	  lemma --- and no sectionwise flatness enters. The local-gluing description below
1484	  records the equivalent concrete local realization through the
1485	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
1486	
1487	  Concretely, the same isomorphism is described by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
1488	  through the restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
1489	  Fix a
1490	  common open cover \(\{U\}\) of \(X\). On each member \(U\) form the canonical
1491	  composite
1492	  \[
1493	    \bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P\bigr)\big|_U
1494	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1495	    \bigl(M|_U \otimes_U N|_U\bigr) \otimes_U P|_U
1496	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1497	    M|_U \otimes_U \bigl(N|_U \otimes_U P|_U\bigr)
1498	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1499	    \bigl(M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\big|_U ,
1500	  \]
1501	  in which:
1502	  \begin{itemize}
1503	    \item the first arrow is two applications of the restriction-compatibility
1504	      isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} along the open immersion
1505	      \(U \hookrightarrow X\): first \((M \otimes_X N)|_U \cong M|_U \otimes_U
1506	      N|_U\) tensored with \(P|_U\), then \(\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X
1507	      P\bigr)|_U \cong (M \otimes_X N)|_U \otimes_U P|_U\);
1508	    \item the middle arrow is the \emph{canonical presheaf-level associator}
1509	      \(\alpha\) of the (symmetric) monoidal category
1510	      \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\)
1511	      (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\),
1512	      \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)), read on the restricted modules;
1513	    \item the third arrow is again two applications of
1514	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, in the opposite direction.
1515	  \end{itemize}
1516	  Each of the three arrows is a \emph{canonical} isomorphism: the
1517	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is induced
1518	  by the open immersion with no choices, hence natural in its module arguments, and
1519	  the presheaf associator \(\alpha\) is a fixed natural transformation. Therefore,
1520	  on a double overlap \(U \cap U'\), restricting the \(U\)-composite and the
1521	  \(U'\)-composite to \(U \cap U'\) yields the same morphism: each of the three
1522	  arrows commutes with the further restriction to \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1523	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the presheaf associator, so the two local
1524	  composites agree on the overlap.
1525	
1526	  Because the internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(-,-)\) of sheaves of
1527	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is itself a sheaf on \(X\), a family of morphisms defined
1528	  on the members of an open cover and agreeing on the overlaps glues to a single
1529	  global morphism. Applying this both to the local composites above and to their
1530	  local inverses produces a global morphism
1531	  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \to M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\) whose restriction
1532	  to each \(U\) is the local composite, together with a two-sided inverse glued from
1533	  the local inverses; hence the glued morphism is a global isomorphism --- the
1534	  associator. The single non-formal input of this re-route is the
1535	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the presheaf
1536	  associator (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)) and the sheaf-of-modules
1537	  internal-hom gluing are already available.
1538	
1539	  Note that, unlike the pointwise-existence statement
1540	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} --- a \emph{proposition} proved
1541	  cover-pointwise with \emph{no} overlap obligation --- the associator is
1542	  \emph{global data}, a single isomorphism of sheaves on all of \(X\). Assembling it
1543	  from local pieces is therefore genuinely stronger than that pattern: the
1544	  overlap-naturality compatibility of the local composites must be discharged before
1545	  gluing, and is discharged above. The locally-trivial hypotheses of the statement
1546	  are not consumed; the comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is applied to the
1547	  global, untrivialised restrictions \((M \otimes_X N)|_U\), so no free-cover
1548	  specialisation can replace it. What this proof does \emph{not} require is the
1549	  \emph{full} \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance or the
1550	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category
1551	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W,lem:jw_ismonoidal}): only the single left-whisker obligation
1552	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed via the d.2 stalk--tensor
1553	  commutation \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}, is consumed by the sheafification
1554	  transport. The group law on \(\otimes\)-iso-classes
1555	  consumes only the \emph{existence} of the resulting isomorphism, never its pentagon,
1556	  triangle, or naturality coherence.
1557	\end{proof}

1567	  structure-sheaf unit object, there are natural isomorphisms
1568	  \[

1763	  \emph{locally-trivial} subtype
1764	  \[

1800	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.LineBundle.OnProduct.pullback_tensorObj_iso}
1801	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1802	  % NOTE: pullback preserves the structure sheaf and tensor products (and hence
1803	  % tensor-invertibility) by the standard pullback-tensor compatibility of
1804	  % \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules (Stacks \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} /
1805	  % \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}), independently of the open-immersion
1806	  % restriction-compatibility iso.
1807	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
1808	  functor
1809	  \[
1810	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
1811	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
1812	  \]
1813	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
1814	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
1815	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
1816	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
1817	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
1818	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
1819	  canonical isomorphism
1820	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
1821	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
1822	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
1823	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.

1826	\begin{proof}
1827	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1828	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
1829	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
1830	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
1831	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
1832	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
1833	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
1834	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\): on each
1835	  affine open where \(\mathcal{N}\) and \(\mathcal{N}'\) are free of rank one
1836	  over \(A\), both sides evaluate to the free rank-one \(B\)-module, so
1837	  the comparison is an isomorphism. No flatness of \(A \to B\) is required
1838	  at this level, as the modules involved are locally free. The compatibility
1839	  with the unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
1840	  same cover.

1843	\section{The varying-ring stalk--tensor commutation (d.2) and the unconditional
1844	left-whiskering}
1845	\label{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}
1846	
1847	This section supplies the one genuinely Mathlib-absent ingredient on which the
1848	\emph{unconditional} associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} rests: the commutation
1849	of the stalk functor with the relative-ring tensor product of presheaves of modules
1850	(``d.2''). Together with the already-built \(R_x\)-linear stalk comparison
1851	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, ``d.1'') it closes the single open left-whiskering
1852	obligation for an \emph{arbitrary} whiskering object --- with no flatness and no
1853	local-triviality hypothesis --- which is exactly what makes the sheafification
1854	transport of the presheaf associator work for all modules. The construction is to be
1855	formalized in the dedicated Lean file
1856	\(\mathtt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/StalkTensor.lean}\).
1857	
1858	\begin{lemma}
1859	
1860	\leanok
1861	[Stalk of a tensor product (d.2): varying-ring stalk--tensor commutation]
1862	  \label{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}
1863	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorIso}
1864	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map}
1865	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Tensor product),
1866	  % Lemma lemma-stalk-tensor-product (stalk of a tensor product of modules is the
1867	  % tensor product of the stalks over the stalk ring)
1868	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2332-L2344).
1869	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1870	  % "\begin{lemma}
1871	  % \label{lemma-stalk-tensor-product}
1872	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1873	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
1874	  % Let $x \in X$. There is a canonical isomorphism
1875	  % of $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$-modules
1876	  % $$
1877	  % (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})_x
1878	  % =
1879	  % \mathcal{F}_x \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{X, x}} \mathcal{G}_x
1880	  % $$
1881	  % functorial in $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$.
1882	  % \end{lemma}"
1883	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces,
1884	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-stalk-tensor-product}.}
1885	  Let \(R : (\mathtt{Opens}\,X)^{op} \to \mathtt{CommRingCat}\) be the structure
1886	  presheaf of a scheme \(X\), and let \(A, B\) be presheaves of
1887	  \(R\)-modules (objects of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,(R \circ
1888	  \mathtt{forget_2}\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat})\)). For each point
1889	  \(x \in X\) there is a canonical isomorphism of \((R \circ
1890	  \mathtt{forget_2}).\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-modules
1891	  \[
1892	    (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x
1893	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1894	    A.\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\otimes_{R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x}\; B.\mathtt{stalk}\,x,
1895	  \]
1896	  natural in \(A\) and \(B\), where \(\otimes^{p}\) is the presheaf-of-modules tensor
1897	  product \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\).
1898	\end{lemma}
1899	
1900	\begin{proof}
1901	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map}
1902	  \leanok
1903	  The stalk at \(x\) is the filtered colimit over the neighbourhoods \(U \ni x\) of
1904	  the sections functor, and sectionwise the tensor product is computed pointwise:
1905	  \((A \otimes^{p} B)(U) = A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)\)
1906	  (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj\_obj}\)). The neighbourhood system
1907	  \(\{U \ni x\}\) is cofiltered under inclusion, so the colimit is filtered. The
1908	  isomorphism is assembled in five named stages. Stages (i)--(ii) construct the
1909	  forward additive comparison map and its germ characterisation; stages (iii)--(v)
1910	  upgrade it to an \(R_x\)-linear map, construct the reverse map, and check mutual
1911	  inversion.
1912	
1913	  \emph{(i) Per-neighbourhood balanced bilinear map and its section-level descent}
1914	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorBilin}\), \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}\)). For a fixed
1915	  neighbourhood \(U \ni x\) the assignment
1916	  \[
1917	    (a, b) \;\longmapsto\; \mathrm{germ}_x\,a \,\otimes\, \mathrm{germ}_x\,b,
1918	    \qquad a \in A(U),\ b \in B(U),
1919	  \]
1920	  is \(R(U)\)-balanced bilinear into \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\): it is additive in each
1921	  variable, and for \(r \in R(U)\) the two ways of moving \(r\) across the tensor agree
1922	  after passing to germs, since \(\mathrm{germ}_x(r \cdot a) = (\mathrm{germ}_x r)
1923	  \cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a\) and the stalk tensor is balanced over
1924	  \(R_x = R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) (the germ--scalar compatibility of
1925	  \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}). By the universal property of the relative tensor
1926	  product it descends to an additive map on the section module
1927	  \[
1928	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}_U \;\colon\;
1929	      (A \otimes^{p} B)(U) = A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)
1930	        \;\longrightarrow\; A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x,
1931	    \qquad a \otimes b \longmapsto \mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x b.
1932	  \]
1933	
1934	  \emph{(ii) Colimit descent to the forward comparison map}
1935	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\), \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)). The
1936	  maps \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}_U\) are compatible with the restriction maps of the
1937	  presheaf \(A \otimes^{p} B\) as \(U\) shrinks toward \(x\) (a smaller neighbourhood
1938	  computes the same germs), so they form a cocone over the filtered diagram
1939	  \(\{(A \otimes^{p} B)(U)\}_{U \ni x}\). The induced map out of the colimit is the
1940	  forward additive comparison map
1941	  \[
1942	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc} \;\colon\;
1943	      (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\longrightarrow\; A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x,
1944	  \]
1945	  characterised on germs of simple tensors by
1946	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\bigl(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes b)\bigr) =
1947	  \mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x b\)
1948	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)). This is the forward half of the asserted
1949	  isomorphism (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_desc_forward}).
1950	
1951	  \emph{(iii) \(R_x\)-linearity packaging} (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorLinearMap}\)). The
1952	  additive map of (ii) is upgraded to an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear map: on a germ
1953	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x r\) of a section \(r \in R(U)\) one checks
1954	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\bigl((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \xi\bigr) =
1955	  (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}(\xi)\) on germs of simple tensors
1956	  and extends by additivity. The one subtlety is a \emph{carrier-duality obstacle}:
1957	  the section-level tensor \(A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)\) is a module over the
1958	  \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier \((R \circ \mathtt{forget_2})(U)\), whereas the natural
1959	  scalar \(r : R(U)\) lives in the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) carrier \(R(U)\); the two
1960	  carriers are canonically identified but not definitionally equal, so the
1961	  scalar-pulling lemma \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.smul\_tmul'}\) applies only after
1962	  inserting a canonical \(\mathtt{RingEquiv}\) bridge between the
1963	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carriers. Once that bridge is
1964	  in place the linearity computation mirrors the germ-representative pattern used
1965	  for the d.1 stalk map \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) (\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}).
1966	
1967	  \emph{(iv) The reverse map.} The map
1968	  \[
1969	    A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \;\longrightarrow\; (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x
1970	  \]
1971	  is built by the universal property of the tensor product over \(R_x\) from the
1972	  \(R_x\)-bilinear map of the two stalks
1973	  \[
1974	    (\mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b) \;\longmapsto\;
1975	      \mathrm{germ}_x\bigl(a|_{U \cap V} \otimes b|_{U \cap V}\bigr),
1976	  \]
1977	  where \(a \in A(U)\) and \(b \in B(V)\) are chosen representatives; this is a
1978	  \emph{nested} colimit descent, descending first the \(A\)-variable out of the
1979	  filtered colimit \(\{A(U)\}_{U \ni x}\) and then the \(B\)-variable out of
1980	  \(\{B(V)\}_{V \ni x}\), passing to the common refinement \(U \cap V\) to form the
1981	  simple tensor and its germ. Well-definedness on germs (independence of the chosen
1982	  representatives and of the neighbourhoods \(U, V\)) is exactly filteredness of the
1983	  neighbourhood system.
1984	
1985	  It remains to check that the reverse map is \(R_x\)-bilinear, and in particular that
1986	  its underlying bilinear map is \emph{balanced} over \(R_x\), i.e. that pulling a scalar
1987	  out of the left variable agrees with pulling it out of the right. The key point is to
1988	  establish this balancing \emph{at the stalk level}, with every scalar living in the
1989	  stalk ring \(R_x = R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) throughout. Concretely, for a germ
1990	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x r\) of a section \(r \in R(W)\) one verifies the identity
1991	  \[
1992	    \mathtt{revBihom}\bigl((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b\bigr)
1993	      \;=\; (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathtt{revBihom}\bigl(\mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b\bigr),
1994	  \]
1995	  and symmetrically on the right variable, where the scalar \(\mathrm{germ}_x r \in R_x\)
1996	  never leaves the stalk ring. This holds because of the germ--scalar compatibility
1997	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x(r \cdot s) = (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot(\mathrm{germ}_x s)\) of the germ
1998	  map \(R(W) \to R_x\) (the Lean lemma \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\)) — the same germ--scalar
1999	  compatibility already used in stages (i) and (iii). Multiplying a representative
2000	  \(r \cdot a\) and then taking germs produces \((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a\),
2001	  so the action of \(r\) is transparently the \(R_x\)-action on the stalk and may be
2002	  moved freely across the tensor of stalks, where balancedness over \(R_x\) is built into
2003	  \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\). Because the whole computation takes place over the single
2004	  ring \(R_x\), no carrier identification intervenes and the balancing follows directly.
2005	
2006	  One must \emph{not} instead reduce this balancing to a section-level identity. If the
2007	  scalar were pushed through the presheaf restriction to a common neighbourhood \(W\) and
2008	  the balancing read off inside the section module \(A(W) \otimes_{R(W)} B(W)\), then the
2009	  scalar action appearing there is the one produced by the presheaf-of-modules structure
2010	  of \(A\) via restriction of scalars: it lives over the \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier
2011	  \((R \circ \mathtt{forget_2})(W)\), \emph{not} over the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) carrier
2012	  \(R(W)\) that annotates the section tensor. This is precisely the
2013	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-vs-\(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier-duality obstacle already met in
2014	  stage (iii), here compounded by the extra restriction-of-scalars wrapper, so the naive
2015	  ``move the scalar across the tensor'' step (\(\mathtt{TensorProduct.smul\_tmul'}\)) does
2016	  not apply at the section level without first inserting the carrier bridge. Working at the
2017	  stalk level as above sidesteps this wrapper entirely.
2018	
2019	  Where a \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-vs-\(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier identification is
2020	  genuinely unavoidable, it is the \emph{same} canonical bridge already invoked for the
2021	  stage-(iii) \(R_x\)-linearity packaging (the \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU\_smul}\)/%
2022	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorLinearMap}\) step, \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_linear_map}): the two
2023	  carriers are canonically identified but not definitionally equal, and the same
2024	  \(\mathtt{RingEquiv}\) bridge resolves the obstruction here. Thus the stage-(iv)
2025	  balancing reuses the technique already resolved in stage (iii) rather than re-deriving
2026	  it.
2027	
2028	  \emph{(v) Mutual inversion on germ generators} (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\)). The two
2029	  composites are checked to be identities on generators. The composite
2030	  \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \to (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \to A_x
2031	  \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) is the identity on a simple tensor \(\mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes
2032	  \mathrm{germ}_x b\) by the germ characterisation \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)
2033	  of (ii), and extends to all of \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) by
2034	  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.induction\_on}\). The opposite composite
2035	  \((A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \to A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \to (A \otimes^{p}
2036	  B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) is the identity on a germ \(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes b)\) of
2037	  a simple tensor by the same characterisation; since germs generate the stalk and the
2038	  germ maps are jointly epimorphic (\(\mathtt{stalk\_hom\_ext}\)), together with
2039	  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.induction\_on}\) applied to each section, the composite is the
2040	  identity. Bundling the \(R_x\)-linear forward map of (iii) with the reverse map of
2041	  (iv) and these two inversion identities yields the asserted isomorphism
2042	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\).
2043	
2044	  Naturality in \(A, B\) is inherited from naturality of the sectionwise tensor and of
2045	  the germ maps. This is the presheaf-of-modules, varying-ring analogue of the Stacks
2046	  lemma above; the only subtlety beyond the ringed-space statement is that the ground
2047	  ring varies with \(U\), handled by the ``tensor of filtered colimits over the colimit
2048	  ring'' identification together with the carrier-duality bridge of (iii). Conceptually
2049	  the tensor product of modules commutes with filtered colimits in each variable and
2050	  the ground ring \(R(U)\) itself colimits to the stalk ring \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\),
2051	  which is the reason the forward map (ii) and the reverse map (iv) are mutually
2052	  inverse.
2053	\end{proof}
2054	
2055	\begin{lemma}
2056	
2057	\leanok

2069	  characterised on germs of simple tensors by

2079	
2080	\begin{lemma}
2081	
2082	\leanok
2083	[\(R_x\)-linear stalk--tensor comparison map (d.2, stage (iii))]
2084	  \label{lem:stalk_tensor_linear_map}
2085	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorLinearMap}
2086	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_tensor_desc_forward, lem:stalk_linear_map}
2087	  With \(R\), \(A\), \(B\), \(x\) as in \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}, the

2107	The proof of the live whiskering lemma below factors through two named
2108	sub-lemmas: the \emph{d.1-bridge} relating membership in the sheafification
2109	localizer \(J.W\) to stalkwise isomorphism, and the \emph{second-factor
2110	naturality} of the d.2 commutation isomorphism \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}.
2111	We state both before the lemma that consumes them.
2112	
2113	\begin{lemma}
2114	
2115	\leanok
2116	[d.1-bridge: locally bijective implies stalkwise iso]
2117	  \label{lem:W_implies_stalkwise_iso}
2118	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.isIso_stalkFunctor_map_of_W}
2119	  \uses{lem:stalk_linear_map}
2120	  Let \(X\) be a topological space and \(R\) a presheaf of commutative rings on the
2121	  small site \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), equipped with its canonical Grothendieck topology
2122	  \(J\) satisfying \(J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}\). Let
2123	  \(g : M \to N\) be a morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules whose underlying
2124	  morphism of \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-valued presheaves lies in \(J.W\). Then for every point
2125	  \(x \in X\) the induced \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map
2126	  \[
2127	    (\mathtt{stalkFunctor}\,\mathtt{Ab}\,x).\mathtt{map}\,
2128	      \bigl((\mathtt{toPresheaf}).\mathtt{map}\,g\bigr)
2129	    \;\colon\; M_x \;\longrightarrow\; N_x
2130	  \]
2131	  is an isomorphism. Conversely, if the \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map is an isomorphism at
2132	  every point, then \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}).\mathtt{map}\,g \in J.W\).
2133	\end{lemma}
2134	
2135	\begin{proof}
2136	  \uses{lem:stalk_linear_map}
